title: Dog Vomiting Bright Green Substance: Emergency Triage Guide
meta_desc: Is your dog vomiting bright green? Learn why this is a high-urgency emergency and why you should contact a vet immediately.
content_html:
TL;DR: Bright green vomit in dogs is a high-priority emergency that may indicate life-threatening toxin ingestion or gastrointestinal blockages. Seek immediate veterinary care and take a photo of the vomit to help the medical team quickly identify the cause.

What does it mean if my dog vomits a bright green substance?

Bright green vomit in dogs typically indicates the presence of bile, a digestive fluid produced by the liver and stored in the gallbladder. While bile is naturally yellow-green, a vibrant or neon green color often suggests that the substance has moved very quickly through the digestive tract or that the dog has ingested something specific, such as:

  • Rodenticides (rat poison)
  • Toxic plants
  • Blue-green algae

Is bright green vomit in dogs a medical emergency?

Yes. The urgency level for this symptom is High. Because bright green vomiting can be a primary clinical sign of life-threatening issues, you should seek immediate veterinary care for conditions including:

  • Gastrointestinal blockages
  • Severe pancreatitis
  • Ingestion of neurotoxic rat poisons

Do not wait for a "wait and see" approach if the vomit is persistent or accompanied by lethargy and loss of appetite.

How does taking a photo of my dog's vomit help with triage?

If it is safe to do so, take a clear photo of the vomit to show your veterinarian. Visual evidence is vital for the medical team to:

  • Assess the exact shade and consistency of the fluid
  • Differentiate between simple bile, undigested grass, or chemical dyes found in certain poisons
  • Prioritize your dog's case during the triage process
  • Prepare the correct diagnostics or antidotes upon your arrival

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Vomiting of sudden onset may contain bile or evidence of ingested substances such as grass. Causes of vomiting include dietary indiscretion (ingestion of novel, spoiled, or contaminated foods, or foreign material), drug or toxin ingestion (antibiotics, NSAIDs, corticosteroids, plants, chemicals), systemic illness (pancreatitis, uremic gastropathy, hypoadrenocorticism), endoparasitism, or bacterial or viral infection. In cases of acute vomiting, oral intake should be discontinued for up to 24 hours, followed by small amounts of a bland, low-fat, easily digestible diet introduced frequently, with gradual transition to the usual diet over 3-5 days. Antiemetic drugs should be used to control vomiting only after an etiologic diagnosis has been made or if vomiting is protracted or severe enough to cause dehydration or electrolyte imbalances; options include metoclopramide, maropitant, and ondansetron.

Chapter: Gastroenterology, Toxicology, Infectious Disease

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 387)

faqs.question: Can eating grass cause bright green vomit?
faqs.answer: While grass contains chlorophyll that can tint vomit, grass-related vomiting usually contains visible blades of plant matter. Liquid, neon-green vomit is more likely to be bile or a chemical toxin.

faqs.question: Is green vomit always a sign of poisoning?
faqs.answer: Not always, but it is a common sign of certain types of rat poisons (rodenticides). Because the risk is so high, vets treat bright green vomit as a potential toxicity until proven otherwise.

faqs.question: What should I do if my vet is closed?
faqs.answer: Since this is a high-urgency situation, you should immediately contact the nearest 24-hour emergency animal hospital.
